data.title: Bristol Myers Squibb: Strong Pipeline, Weak Outlook (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
About a year ago, I published my last article about the pharmaceutical company Bristol Myers Squibb Company (BMY), and I rated the stock as a “Buy” and also wrote that it is one of the better picks in the current market. I also wrote in my conclusion:

We certainly should keep the disappointing study results in mind, but Bristol Myers Squibb raising guidance despite the challenging macroeconomic environment is a good sign in my opinion.

Of course, the risk always remains that the stock will drop even lower – and as I am writing this, investors seem not completely happy with the results and/or the guidance as the stock declined about 2%. But considering the limited risk for pharmaceutical companies in case of a recession and in light of tariffs as well as the low valuation multiples the stock is currently trading for, this is a risk we can take in my opinion. And even if we have to wait before the stock price moves higher, we can at least collect dividends in the meantime and a dividend yield above 5% is certainly tempting.

Since my last article was published, the stock increased slightly above 21% (and when including dividends, investors gained even 27.5% in value). This is without much doubt a very good performance over one year, but we also must point out that Bristol Myers Squibb was probably not one of the better picks (as I wrote in my last article). The S&P 500 actually increased 29.6% in the same timeframe and therefore outperformed Bristol Myers Squibb (although the S&P 500 performance was mostly driven by only a few companies).

In the following article, we will analyze that Bristol Myers Squibb has the potential to grow its revenue in the years to come and is trading for a very cheap valuation multiple. But we also must take into account that analysts are very pessimistic about the business and assume declining revenue for several years in a row. In the end, I will argue that Bristol Myers Squibb is still a solid investment, but not the compelling “Buy” it was a year ago.

Quarterly Results

We start by looking at the last results for fiscal 2025, which the company did report in early February 2026. For fiscal 2025, Bristol Myers Squibb reported a total revenue of $48,194, resulting in a decline of 0.2% year-over-year compared to $48,300 million in the previous year. While revenue was declining a bit, the company could now report $9,328 million in EBIT again—following $8,379 million in losses before income taxes in fiscal 2024. And instead of a loss per diluted share of $4.41 in fiscal 2024, the company reported earnings per share of $3.46.

Full-year results are certainly not so great, but fourth-quarter results look a bit brighter. Revenue increased from $12,342 million in Q4/24 to $12,502 million in Q4/25, resulting in 1.3% year-over-year growth. EBIT jumped from only $175 million in the same quarter last year to $1,470 million in this quarter, and diluted earnings per share also increased from $0.04 in Q4/24 to $0.53 in Q4/25. In both cases, we must state that Q4/24 was probably an outlier.

Can BMY Still Grow?

In the last few years, management always acknowledged that Bristol Myers Squibb is facing a patent cliff and that several pharmaceuticals will report declining revenue in the next few years. The company labeled these products its legacy portfolio and always made clear that revenue for this portfolio will decline. On the other hand, the company had its growth portfolio consisting of newer products that will drive growth and at least offset declines from the legacy portfolio.

In 2025 we clearly saw the shift from legacy portfolio to growth portfolio continuing. In fiscal 2024, the biggest part of revenue still stemmed from the legacy portfolio, and in fiscal 2025 the biggest part of revenue now stems from the growth portfolio. Nevertheless, the legacy portfolio is still generating 45% of total revenue, and this still represents a significant risk for declining revenues.

And what seems even more troubling is the fact that Bristol Myers Squibb seems to be able to offset declining revenue from its legacy portfolio, but the growth portfolio can’t drive overall revenue higher. For fiscal 2026, total revenue is expected to be between $46.0 billion and $47.5 billion, which would result in low-single-digit declines once again. Diluted earnings per share are also expected to be in a range of $6.05 to $6.35, which would result either in a low single digit decline or low-single-digit growth year-over-year.

Management is only offering guidance for fiscal 2026, but analysts are presenting earnings and revenue expectations for several years to come. Bristol Myers Squibb is usually trying to be optimistic that new products will set off declines, and the business will also be able to grow, but analysts are obviously not buying it.

Revenue outlook for the next few years seems rather grim. It is not surprising that analysts are often cautious about pharmaceutical companies and do not necessarily assume high growth rates in 5 or 10 years from now. Growth is heavily dependent on the ability of these companies to introduce new products to the market and not every new pharmaceutical turns out to be a blockbuster.

However, in case of Bristol Myers Squibb, the analysts are expecting revenue to constantly decline in the next few years. Compared to a revenue of slightly above $48 billion in fiscal 2025, analysts are only expecting $36 billion in revenue in fiscal 2030 – resulting in an annual decline of 5.6%. These predictions are also not based on just one or two analysts, but many different people being quite pessimistic about Bristol Myers Squibb. This is implying that analysts are not really optimistic about the company’s current growth portfolio.

It is also implying that analysts are not really believing in the pipeline and the company’s ability to introduce new additional blockbusters to the market in the next few years. Management is seeing this different and pointing towards its pipeline and is talking about a wave of at least 10 new product launch opportunities until 2030 and is presenting a pipeline with almost 70 candidates.

Among these pipeline candidates, management is pointing towards five more promising candidates – including Cobenfy, Milvexian, Admilparant, Pumitamig and Iberdomide as well as Mezigdomide.

Cobenfy already generated $155 million in fiscal 2025 and $50 million in Q4/25. And with more data being expected in 2027 and 2028 it might be used for many different indications leading to peak sales of $4 billion to $5 billion and about 10 million potential patients. During the last earnings call, management commented:

Cobenfy revenue in the fourth quarter was $51 million, with continued steady uptake among prescribers and patients. Cobenfy's uptake has surpassed all schizophrenia comparators and relevant analogs in the first year of launch, and we continue to expect steady growth throughout the year.

A second promising drug is Milvexian, for which management is expecting about 15 million potential patients. The company is expecting more data in 2026 and analysts are still very optimistic and expect peak sales to be at least $1.5 billion (in the past, analysts have been more optimistic and expected sales to be somewhere between $3 billion and $5 billion).

Finally, we can mention Pumitamig, also known as BNT327, which is developed under a strategic partnership with the German company BioNTech (BNTX). Not only did both companies report promising phase II results, but analysts are also expecting peak sales of $4.5 billion.

All in all, Bristol Myers Squib has the theoretical potential to grow revenue in the years to come and certainly set off the declining sales from its legacy portfolio. But we never know whether these products actually make it to market and what actual sales will be in the years to come.

Dividend

Finally, Bristol Myers Squibb increased its dividend once again – for the 17th consecutive time. And although the dividend was increased only by one cent, the company is now paying $0.63 per share per quarter, which is resulting in an annual dividend of $2.52 and a dividend yield of almost 4.3% making the stock certainly interesting for dividend investors (although the dividend yield has been much higher).

The dividend can also be seen as well covered at this point. When comparing the dividend to the GAAP earnings per share of $3.46, we get a payout ratio of 73%, which is rather high but still acceptable. However, when comparing the dividend to the expected non-GAAP earnings per share of $6.20 for fiscal 2026 (mid-point of guidance), we get a payout ratio of 41%. Finally, we can also compare the amount paid in dividends in fiscal 2025 (about $5.05 billion) to the generated free cash flow ($12.85 billion). This is resulting in a payout ratio of 39% and those metrics are certainly acceptable.

In the years 2019 till 2023, the company also spent high amounts on share buybacks (as high as $8 billion in 2022) and reduced the number of diluted shares outstanding. However, in the last few quarters, the company stopped share repurchase programs.

Balance Sheet

Finally, when considering that analysts are rather pessimistic, we should also look at the company’s balance sheet again. In my last two articles, I did not really take a closer look at the balance sheet, but I think it is important and we should look at the numbers once again.

Let’s start with the good news. The company reduced its total debt from $49,650 million one year earlier to $45,111 million on December 31, 2025. This results in a decrease of 9.1% year-over-year. And the good news is that this was not done by using cash on the balance sheet but by generated free cash flow (which is a good sign).

On December 31, 2025, the company had $2,261 million in short-term debt as well as $42,850 million in long-term debt. When comparing the total debt to the shareholder’s equity of $18,473 million, we get a debt-equity ratio of 2.44, which is rather high. When comparing the total debt to free cash flow of the last four quarters ($12.85 billion) it would take about 3.5 times the free cash flow to repay the debt outstanding. Additionally, we should not ignore $10,673 million in cash and cash equivalents on the balance sheet, that could be used to repay outstanding debt.

If Bristol Myers Squibb is able to grow again, the debt levels are not a problem. However, if analysts are right and the company will rather report declining revenue, these debt levels could become a problem. The business also has $21,754 million in goodwill on its balance sheet – and this amount is exceeding the stockholder’s equity of $18,473 million.

We must not really be worried about the company, its balance sheet and the ability to repay its outstanding debt. But we should keep a close eye on the balance sheet. A combination of declining free cash flow, the debt levels and the high amount of goodwill, could become a problem in the years to come.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

We can start by looking at the simple valuation multiples, Bristol Myers Squibb was trading for in the last few years. And I am looking only at the last five years and I excluded the previous years as there are some spikes for both valuation multiples that would disturb the picture.

At the time of writing, Bristol Myers Squibb is trading for 17.1 times earnings per share and for 9.4 times free cash flow. And especially the P/FCF ratio is indicating that Bristol Myers Squibb might be undervalued at this point. And although the stock was trading for even lower valuation multiples (only about 5.7 times free cash flow in late 2025), a single valuation multiple still seems to make this a very good investment.

When using a discount cash flow calculation, we come to a similar conclusion. As always, we are calculating with a discount rate of 10% (the annual return we like to achieve at least) and with 2,041 million in diluted shares outstanding. As basis for our calculation, we can use the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $12.85 billion. Free cash flow margin is 26.7% right now, which seems to be rather high and clearly above the long-term average of 18.5%. However, in the last ten years, the free cash flow margin was 25.0% on average and in the last five years it was 26.7% and therefore we can argue that the current free cash flow might be reasonable.

Now, let’s assume that Bristol Myers Squibb won’t be able to grow its free cash flow again and assume 0% growth from now till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of $62.96 for Bristol Myers Squibb and the stock would be almost fairly valued at this point.

Additionally, we can also make two different calculations – one that is more optimistic, one that is more pessimistic. In a first scenario, we can assume that Bristol Myers Squibb will be able to grow at least in the low single digits in line with GDP growth. When assuming 3% growth annually (and all other assumptions being similar) we get an intrinsic value of $89.94 for Bristol Myers Squibb. This would make the stock still a bargain at this point.

However, we can also calculate by using analysts’ assumptions. As mentioned above, they are expecting revenue to decline about 5% annually for the next few years and assume the business will stabilize in 2030 again. This leads to an intrinsic value of $53.65, and the stock would be overvalued at this point.

Upcoming Quarter

On April 30, 2026, Bristol Myers Squibb will report first quarter results for fiscal 2026, and analysts are expecting revenue to be $10.93 billion, which would result in about a 2.5% year-over-year decline again. Earnings per share (on a GAAP basis) are expected to be $1.15, and compared to the same quarter last year, this would also result in a 5% year-over-year decline.

Analysts are obviously expecting revenue and earnings per share to remain under pressure, and therefore we should pay close attention to the question of whether the growth portfolio is able to outpace the losses from the legacy portfolio. Especially announcements about the pipeline are important and whether Bristol Myers Squibb will be able to introduce additional blockbusters to the market. Right now, the company has several pipeline candidates, but it also needs to convince analysts and investors that these pharmaceuticals can contribute to a growing revenue.

We should also pay attention to the company’s debt levels and whether the business is able to reduce debt levels as well as goodwill.

Conclusion

Determining whether Bristol Myers Squibb is a good investment or not is rather difficult at this point. On the one hand, we have a company with a growth portfolio and a promising pipeline that might not only be able to offset declines from pharmaceuticals losing patent protection but actually grow revenue in the years to come. On the other hand, we have analysts being rather pessimistic and expecting revenue to decline in the mid-single digits in the years to come.

I personally still see Bristol Myers Squibb as a bit undervalued, but it is not a clear “Buy” anymore. The combination of a rather high dividend yield and the very low valuation multiples the stock is trading for still makes it a solid investment, but now there might be better investments in the market.

data.title: Monarch Casino: The Crown Is Earned, Now It Needs A Kingdom To Conquer (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My thesis on Monarch Casino (MCRI) has aged like fine wine, don't you think?

This top casino pick of mine has delivered an incredible ~58% in less than two years and is still sitting in a pile of cash ready to be used for regional business scoops beyond Reno and Black Hawk.

If you haven't read my last article, I dug into this optionality for Monarch.

But here I want to set aside the M&A thesis for a moment and try to figure out if today's pop was an overreaction or not.

After all, my price target of $100 to $105 was beaten by ~9% after today's movement.

All I want to know is whether the organic growth thesis supports this price or not. And if so, is it still a 'Buy' counting on the M&A optionality (as it was last time) or is there organic growth ahead?

I know, I know. Lots of questions. But I'll try to answer them along the way.

To get things started, I think it's best to take a look at what happened this quarter before making any predictions for the entire FY 2026.

Still The Monarch of Their Markets

That Reno, Black Hawk, and other smaller markets are outperforming the darling Strip this year is no surprise to anyone. If you line up the ten largest U.S. casino markets against the smaller ones, there’s roughly a 100 bp gap in GGR growth.

Needless to say, I'm excluding Chicagoland—an anomaly of FY 2025—from the comparison.

But I'm only going to give you three pieces of information that I typically use as a proxy for traffic on Monarch properties, and then I'll show you how they outperformed it.

I want you to pay close attention here:

  • Black Hawk's AGP was approximately $71.7 million in January and $70.3 million in February (a strong 14% increase in January and 11.8% in February). These two months were actually the city's softest performers of the past year.
  • Denver Airport (another great proxy for Black Hawk traffic, which is about a 50-minute drive away) recorded flat domestic arrivals year-to-date (through February) compared to last year, but international arrivals increased by about 5.4%.
  • Reno's GGR grew 2.9% in January (outperforming the ~6% drop in Nevada's overall GGR) and 7.7% in February. In other words, Washoe County is as strong (or even stronger) than last year, perhaps second only to Boulder.

Strong data. I'm with you on this.

Now let's take a peek at Monarch.

To begin with, it's immediately clear that they outperformed in Gaming (~9.5% bump in Q1 to $79.7 million).

But that's not all. Monarch saw higher revenues in Hotels (~13%), F&B (~5.6%), and even in 'Others' (~7%), which generally includes parking, spa revenue, and other items.

Overall, revenue increased almost 9% to $136.6 million, the highest in two years.

You can see this better here:

A lot of folks probably say that, but they don't realize that for Monarch to achieve this, it's only through promotions or simply having better assets.

And it's easy to tell which is the real, you know?

Just look at the margins.

If they start pulling back, that's them easing up on price just to bring people through the door. But if they hold steady or stretch a little wider, that's a clean share they're taking.

Here too I'll start more gradually expand my scope.

Monarch reported an EBITDA margin of 36.5%, expanding it by more than 500 bp year-over-year.

That's impressive, as their own 5-year average is 32.5%. Compare them to any other top-notch operator like Boyd Gaming (BYD) and you'll see thinner EBITDA margins.

On top of owning their backyard in markets that don't have a ton of competitors, you can also see margins creeping up as the top line takes off and those fixed costs get spread over a much bigger revenue base.

Here's a peek at the margins for each segment:

If all margins are expanding, that's pricing power.

It doesn't get any simpler than that.

On the bottom line, Monarch pulled a net margin of ~20.2%.

Again, above the 5-year average thanks to a balance sheet free of long-term debt. So, since they are no longer spending on heavy Capex, all the incremental dollars are going either to shareholders in buybacks and dividends or to the cash pile they are sitting on.

FY 2026: Business as Usual or Breakthrough Year?

As I've told you in other write-ups, Monarch has two (or even more) paths ahead. But essentially, they can sit tight and keep funneling that extra cash into buybacks, or go out and chase deals.

Given you've probably heard me lay out the roll-up angle before, I'm sticking with the 'steady Monarch' as ​​my base case here.

Oh, and of course. If you want some color into the possible M&A strategy, I recommend reading this article here.

First thing I'd point out is that Reno and Black Hawk started FY 2026 much stronger than FY 2025.

And that's big.

Well, if more people are betting and Monarch has the best assets in the region, it's reasonable to assume they'll take the biggest chunk of that bet money.

But first I want to see what Wall St. is targeting and see if they're missing this or not.

For FY 2026, they're expecting something close to ~$574 million now, up from the $560.5 million they expected pre-earnings. Doing the math here, that's a growth of ~7.7% for the whole year.

In other words, they probably don't expect Monarch to maintain the high single-digit pace for the whole of FY 2026.

I, on the other hand, am a little more optimistic.

I don't think pinpointing something close to $580 million for the entire FY 2026 is an exaggeration, and I even think Wall St. could reach it if Monarch beat expectations again.

Curious if you see it the same way. I'd like to hear your take.

Since we're already talking about market projections, I also want to take a peek at EPS.

Wall St. expects $6.65 here. Plugging in a net margin of ~19% for the whole year (net income of $106.4 million), the outstanding shares would need to be ~16 million. Almost a 9% buyback yield.

I don't think that's possible.

Something in the low- to mid-single-digit range feels more reasonable here. They're just annualizing that net margin.

For me, it's enough to project a few things: EBITDA, OCF, Capex, and FCF.

That's all I need to imagine how much cash they'll be sitting on by the end of the year and probably how many shares they'll repurchase and retire.

So, for EBITDA, I'll take my revenue projection and plug in a margin of something close to ~35%. This would give me an EBITDA of $203 million (a bump of almost 7% year-over-year).

Simple, right? But now things get more complicated.

To begin with, I'll assume that the EBITDA-to-OCF conversion follows the average of the last few years (~83.5%), giving me an OCF of $169.5 million. For a slightly more conservative scenario, I can take something close to 80% ($162.4 million) to avoid overshooting.

Since normalized Capex (post-reforms) is approximately 5% of revenue, I'm pinpointing something close to $30 to $35 million for the entire FY 2026 (remembering that I'm not aiming for significant Capex for expansions here either).

That's a conservative FCF of approximately $130 million!

I will also maintain the returns on capital as follows:

  • The buyback yield is approximately 3 to 4%, bringing the outstanding shares to 17.27 million. In other words, 630,000 shares were repurchased and retired, which, at an average price of approximately $114, would cost Monarch at least $71.8 million.
  • Dividends flat at $1.20 per share, which would cost Monarch something close to $22 million.

In other words, we're talking about a mid-single-digit shareholder yield that would cost Monarch $93.8 million. If I were the company's CFO, I'd already be thinking that keeping that money parked there would be a problem, since the average 5-year ROA they achieve is ~11.4%.

This could destroy value if you're thinking about capital allocation. But even so, I'll use this scenario for my modeling.

See that ~$36 million would remain from that FCF chunk and would go directly to the cash pile, increasing it to $156 million.

Still a ‘Buy’ For 2026?

Starting from the most basic point possible, with the FCF I just modeled and an EV of $1.92 billion, the stock would be trading at an FCF yield of ~6.7%.

We're off to a good start.

These are strong numbers for casinos, surpassing peers like Boyd (~3.5%) and Red Rock Resorts (RRR) (just under 4%).

And look, there's no distressed debt or anything here. Monarch is growing clean. That and the M&A leverage alone would justify an FCF yield trading at a premium because of optionality.

By separating the same group of peers I like to use for brick-and-mortar casinos like Boyd, Red Rock, PENN Entertainment (PENN), Caesars Entertainment (CZR), and Churchill Downs (CHDN), I arrive at a multiple of ~7.8x EBITDA.

Plugging in the $203 million in EBITDA, $156 million in net cash, and ~17.3 million in outstanding shares, the price target here is ~$101 per share.

Almost the same price target I was aiming for before.

Now, if I plug in a premium of something between 8 and 10x EBITDA for everything I told you about Monarch, that would be $115 at the midpoint.

Right on target today.

Triangulating this in Graham's Model, I'll use the $6.65 target for FY 2026 EPS (even though I don't 100% agree with it). Just define the growth rate of that EPS.

If I go with the historical rate (10-year CAGR of ~17%), the model gives me a price target of almost $200!

I don't think that's reasonable. Not only do regional markets have a ceiling, but they also won't achieve that much growth through buybacks. It's better to normalize this by the 5-year CAGR (~9.8%), and even then there would be a nice upside to $132, even with the margin of safety.

Now, if I project the CAGR based on Wall Street expectations (~6.5% until FY 2028), I find a price target of $101.20, even with the margin of safety.

In other words, Monarch is trading slightly above the margin of safety (and its peers) due to the much-talked-about optionality, in my opinion.

And here it's difficult not to return to the topic of M&A.

I ask you to imagine a hypothetical scenario with me here.

If they scoop up some distressed regional medium-sized operator (let's say something close to $60 million in EBITDA and ~$400 million in LT debt) at ~6 to 7x EBITDA, Monarch would end up with LT debt on the balance sheet of ~$690 million in net LT debt at midpoint and generating ~$260 million in EBITDA annually.

So far so good, right?

Note that the net LT debt/EBITDA would be ~2.6x, which is safe for companies like Monarch.

But here's the catch.

Monarch could take these distressed assets, as it did with Black Hawk starting in FY 2013, and transform them into top-tier assets, especially if we're talking about smaller markets.

If they achieve approximately 20% efficiency gains in Year 1, that EBITDA would already be almost $75 million, and they would likely reach $300 million by FY 2027.

Considering a tax rate of 25% and interest on this new debt of 6.5%, they could see an increase of up to approximately $2 in EPS after synergies.

The deal 'self-deleverages' and everyone comes out happy.

Can you see that Monarch is already another well-executed M&A call option?

If they scoop up an asset and make it profitable in a few years (buying at the right price, of course), there's still a run for the stock here.

The problem is that we don't know when they'll do it.

For organic growth, I see them trading near fair value today or even slightly above since the optionality is baked in.

So, I'm downgrading to 'Hold' here.

See, I'm still positive on Monarch and think both Black Hawk and Reno will outperform larger markets, but I think the stock will either move sideways or even return to ~$110 in the short term without any catalyst.

data.title: Brinker International: Sell On Misleading Growth And Balance Sheet Struggles
data.description:
Brinker International (EAT) operates in the U.S. casual dining industry, generating revenue through company-owned restaurant sales and franchise royalties. Chili’s and Maggiano’s Little Italy are their staple restaurants that drive the business.

The stock was up about 5x through between 2023 and 2024. It has been volatile, but flat overall since the beginning of 2025. I gave the stock a hold in January, and it has seen a 4% decline (at the time of writing) since that time.

Within the broader restaurant industry, trends are increasingly shaped by price sensitivity, demand for value, and shifting consumer preferences toward convenience and affordability, while cost pressures from labor and commodities remain persistent headwinds. I am switching my view on EAT, and now have the stock as a sell, ahead of its upcoming FY Q3 2026 earnings. Growth has been slow and even the growth they have had may be misleading. Their gross margin lags peers, and their balance sheet continues to deteriorate.

Where is the Growth?

Growth by adding locations is certainly not part of the game plan for EAT. Operating locations have not grown in recent years, and currently sits at 1,627 total locations, lower than a few years ago. The growth plan has been geared more toward operational efficiencies from already operating locations. Foot traffic and organic same store growth was a positive trend for EAT, but both metrics sunk largely last quarter. Same store sales growth moved from 21.4% higher to just 8.6% last quarter. Foot traffic moved from 13.1% to 2.7% in terms of growth QoQ. That’s a bad sign as one of their positive indicators dropped big, and I will be curiously checking how these play out in next week’s earnings.

Adjusted EPS has been ticking higher at a decent pace, but revenue growth has really slowed in the last 5 quarters or so. I think that the earnings growth is misleading and the lack of location expansion, and now possible lowering foot traffic, could really dampen earnings moving forward. The recent EPS growth is going to be hard to sustain, unless there is a dramatic change in store volume or margin advancement.

Weak Margins

EAT presents a net income and EBITDA margin that outpace industry peers, but a gross margin that severely lags behind, making their margin profile look interesting to investors. Food, labor, and in-store costs are keeping gross margin subdued and this is unlikely to change any time soon. There gross margin has improved but is still below 18% and less than half the sector median.

The growth trends EAT was seeing in foot traffic have been helping bolster net income margin, but I am thinking that after last quarters decline, with another possible decrease on the horizon, that net margin could also began to falter. The bottom-line is that I don’t believe EAT is going to have enough, if any margin improvement to help keep their EPS run alive.

Balance Sheet Ugly

EAT has a balance sheet that is has elevated leveraged and remains heavily debt-funded compared to equity. I pointed this out in my last article, and from this angle things have only gotten worse. Their current ratio has dropped to 0.36, as current liabilities are rising faster than assets.

Total debt has been scaling back, but cash and equivalents sit at a quarter of what they were just in 2024 and are now at just $15 million.

From a leverage standpoint, long-term debt relative to capital remains high and the business continues to operate with a structurally leveraged balance sheet typical of asset-heavy restaurant operators, but still a notable risk factor in downturn scenarios. Their debt-to-equity is 4.6x, which is extremely high for a restaurant company. With this type of liquidity position, this would add to the worries I have on EAT growing.

Cost Headwinds

Food and beverage, restaurant labor, and restaurant expenses all add up to 82.5% of sales. Core operating costs are typically high in this industry, but leaving 17.5% to cover other overhead, marketing, depreciation, and taxes, keeps it pretty tight. Slow sales growth, which has been the case, does not leave much room for any more of a swing in food or labor costs. Even another 1-2% rise in food or labor, would sink profits by a lot based on how slim this already is. Chili’s continues to seek new ideas or menu price increases to help offset rising costs. This presents a challenge to keep customers happy by menu items and by keep prices reasonable as customers hope for in the fast casual space.

Risk to Thesis

The biggest risk to my thesis is that EAT is fairly valued. They have a PE ratio of 15x which is 25% lower than the sector. I can’t deny that this is appealing, my input here is that EPS is not going to keep growing like it has been, and I think that the PE ratio for EAT is likely to rise over the next few quarters.

If FY Q3 reports a big bounce back in same store and foot traffic growth, then that could help the earnings advancements continue and fair value to remain. However, I expect this to stay flat or lower, as it was last quarter, and think it is the beginning of a period for stale and weak earnings for the remainder of the year.

Conclusion

I am trying not to be too harsh on EAT, and I do think they have done a fine job with keeping customer engagement based on consistently revamping the menu, specifically at Chili’s. Unfortunately, I just think that the cash position the company is in with very high leverage, and cost headwinds, is going to make it a difficult road ahead.

They have had nice earnings growth, but I think that needs to be taken with a grain of salt, as sales are not keeping up. Q3 adjusted EPS is expected at $2.86, which would be only a 7% increase YoY, already much less than recent prior quarter expansion. I think they may even struggle to reach this mark and the key will be to watch for numbers on organic same store, plus foot traffic growth. I anticipate for this numbers to drop and management tone toward this is critical to keep an eye on. If it is lower again, I think red flags need to flash to investors, especially if sales keeps its pattern as expected with roughly 3% growth YoY. This pattern or trend is not enough and even if current location growth emerges higher again, I still think total growth prospects are too low and that this is eventually going to catch up with earnings and their valuation. I have EAT as a sell.

data.title: Volatility ETF Guide: Why Interest Rate Volatility Matters (Not Just The VIX)
data.description:
Rising tensions in the Middle East have renewed investor interest in volatility ETFs, which are designed to increase in value as expected market volatility rises. While VIX-linked ETFs are well known, they focus on equities and overlook fixed-income and interest-rate volatility, a meaningful component of most portfolios. The Quadratic Interest Rate Volatility and Inflation Hedge ETF (IVOL) is designed to address this gap.

How Does the IVOL ETF Work?

IVOL is managed by Quadratic Capital Management, an asset management firm founded in 2013 by Nancy Davis, which focuses on options and volatility markets.

IVOL was listed on the NYSE in 2019 as a fixed-income completion ETF, designed to address two structural gaps in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index ("the Agg"), the benchmark tracked by most U.S. bond investors.

The Agg contains no inflation protection since there are no TIPS (Treasury Inflation Protected Securities) within the index. This leaves bond investors with a gap in their core fixed income. Another issue for investors arises from the fact that roughly one-third of the Agg is short interest rate options. This short position is due to the Agg’s allocations to mortgage-backed securities and callable corporate bonds. (In the case of mortgages, US homeowners are long the option to prepay their debt. And corporates can call their bonds, often just when bondholders would prefer that they didn’t.) IVOL was designed to complement core bond holdings by seeking to address both of these issues.

The fund combines two core exposures:

  • TIPS and/or cash (at least 80% of the portfolio): U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities provide income that adjusts with the Consumer Price Index (CPI), seeking to help preserve purchasing power in inflationary environments, while serving as the fund's defensive core.
  • Long OTC interest rate options (the remainder): Fully funded, long-only options on the U.S. interest rate swap curve, specifically the spread between 2-year and 10-year swap rates (known as "2s10s"), that seek to benefit from rising fixed income volatility and a steepening yield curve. Because IVOL is long options, it maintains long interest rate volatility exposure.

The yield curve is the relationship between short- and long-term interest rates. When long-term rates rise relative to short-term rates, the yield curve steepens. This dynamic has historically been associated with inflationary environments and, in certain cases, with significant equity market declines, as the Federal Reserve has at times cut short-term rates in response to economic stress.

How Is IVOL Different from a VIX ETF?

VIX-linked ETFs track short-term futures on the CBOE Volatility Index, which measures expected equity market volatility derived from S&P 500 options. They have historically spiked during acute equity selloffs, but they carry a structural cost known as negative roll yield, which causes them to erode over time, making them better suited as short-term tactical tools than long-term portfolio allocations. They also generate no income and offer no exposure to inflation or interest rate volatility, leaving significant gaps for investors seeking broader risk management.

IVOL differs from VIX ETFs in several ways:

  • Different volatility exposure: IVOL targets interest rate volatility through the fixed income options market, not equity volatility through VIX futures. These are distinct risk factors that may respond differently to various market conditions.
  • Income generation: Unlike VIX ETFs, which generate no income and may erode through roll costs, IVOL's TIPS allocation seeks to provide inflation-adjusted monthly income distributions.
  • Inflation exposure: IVOL is designed to provide inflation-linked exposure through its TIPS holdings and swap curve options. VIX ETFs do not offer inflation-linked exposure.
  • Yield curve positioning: IVOL is designed to potentially benefit from yield curve steepening, which can occur both when long-term rates rise and when the Federal Reserve cuts short-term rates in response to economic weakness. VIX ETFs have no yield curve exposure.
  • Long-term portfolio suitability: Because IVOL uses fully funded, long-only OTC options rather than rolling short-dated futures, it is designed to function as a strategic portfolio allocation rather than a short-term trade.

Why Consider IVOL in a Portfolio?

The traditional 60/40 portfolio assumes that bonds will provide stability when equities sell off. As 2022 demonstrated, that relationship is not guaranteed. When inflation is a primary driver of market stress, stocks and bonds can decline together, potentially leaving investors with limited diversification.

IVOL is designed to address this dynamic, seeking to provide a differentiated return profile in environments characterized by rising inflation and rising fixed income volatility. Key potential portfolio considerations include:

  • Inflation exposure: TIPS holdings provide income and principal that adjust with CPI, while swap curve options seek to add market-based inflation expectations beyond what CPI alone captures.
  • Interest rate volatility exposure: Long OTC interest rate options seek to potentially benefit when fixed income volatility rises.
  • Yield curve steepening exposure: Designed to potentially benefit whether the yield curve steepens through rising long-term rates or falling short-term rates.
  • Fixed income complement: IVOL is not designed to replace a core bond allocation but to complement it, seeking to address the inflation and volatility gaps that Agg-based funds may leave open.
  • Institutional market access: IVOL seeks to provide individual investors with exposure to the OTC fixed income options market through a liquid, exchange-traded structure.
  • Defined options cost: The use of long-only, fully funded options means the maximum loss on the options portion is limited to the premium paid.

An investor holding a 60/40 portfolio might consider allocating a portion of the bond sleeve to IVOL, for example, 30% Agg and 10% IVOL. However, the appropriate allocation will depend on each investor's individual circumstances, risk tolerance, and objectives. The right framework for evaluating IVOL is not whether it outperformed the S&P 500 in a given year, but whether it fulfilled its intended role within the broader portfolio.

Key Risks and Considerations

IVOL is not risk-free. Investors should carefully consider the following before allocating:

  • Interest rate risk: The TIPS component is sensitive to changes in real interest rates. If real yields rise significantly, TIPS prices can fall.
  • Inflation risk: If inflation remains low or declines, the benefits of TIPS and inflation-linked exposure may be limited or negative relative to nominal bonds.
  • Options risk: The interest rate options can lose value or expire worthless if the yield curve and rate volatility do not move as anticipated.
  • Cost: Strategies involving OTC options and active management typically carry higher expense ratios than plain-vanilla bond index funds.
  • Strategy risk: IVOL's performance depends on Quadratic's approach to structuring and managing the TIPS and options book. Outcomes can differ from investor expectations.

Conclusion

For investors looking for a volatility ETF that goes beyond equity market hedges, IVOL offers a differentiated approach: inflation-linked income, long interest rate volatility through a market that has historically been largely inaccessible to individual investors, and yield curve positioning that may offer a distinct return profile across a range of market environments. As with any investment, results will vary, and there is no guarantee that IVOL will achieve its investment objectives.

Definitions

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS): U.S. government bonds whose principal value adjusts with changes in the Consumer Price Index (CPI), providing investors with inflation-adjusted income and principal. At maturity, holders receive at least the original principal.

Yield Curve: A line that plots the interest rates of bonds with equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. A steepening yield curve occurs when the gap between short-term and long-term rates widens.

VIX (Cboe Volatility Index): A real-time index that represents the market's expectations for volatility in the S&P 500 over the next 30 days, derived from S&P 500 options prices.

Over-the-Counter (OTC) Market: A decentralized market where financial instruments are traded directly between parties rather than through a formal exchange, historically accessible primarily to large institutional investors.

Roll Yield: The gain or loss generated when a futures contract approaching expiration is replaced with a longer-dated contract. Negative roll yield occurs when longer-dated contracts are more expensive than near-term ones, creating a potential structural cost for investors holding futures-based products over time.

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index ("The Agg"): A broad-based, market capitalization-weighted bond market index representing intermediate-term investment-grade bonds traded in the United States, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, and mortgage-backed securities. Inception date: January 1, 1976.

S&P 500 Index: A market capitalization-weighted index tracking the performance of 500 leading publicly traded companies in the United States, broadly representing the large-cap equity market. Inception date: March 4, 1957.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Oklo: Positioned To Power The AI Infrastructure Boom
data.description:
Oklo Inc.'s (OKLO) investment opportunity stems from a unique combination of technology, business model, and competitive advantages relative to other SMRs. The company has created a vertically integrated SMR with an in-house design for reactors, fuel recycling capabilities, and a behind-the-meter delivery model, which uniquely addresses one of the biggest growth areas in AI compute infrastructure today: access to reliable power.

Its unique ability to recycle nuclear fuels enables it to benefit from lower costs compared to using new uranium, potentially providing protection from volatility in the commodity's prices. Additionally, Oklo is able to deploy its reactor in close proximity to customers and avoid long wait times associated with obtaining a connection to the existing utility grid. Given the rapid expected growth rate of AI-powered computing demand, Oklo will be providing clean energy solutions to data center operators for years to come.

A second key factor driving early interest in Oklo is the size and quality of the agreements signed by the company with major hyperscaler clients, including Meta, along with large non-binding commitments from Switch and Equinix. While most of this potential capacity does not come with the certainty of a binding PPA agreement, even moderate success (deploying 1–2 GW) could result in hundreds of millions of dollars of high-margin EBITDA for Oklo due to the attractive economics of the business.

The company has also established a viable regulatory strategy, which provides a substantial timeline advantage over all other SMR companies. Oklo has chosen to leverage the DOE's Pilot Program instead of pursuing a full license via the NRC, meaning the company will be able to complete construction of its first reactor prior to receiving full commercial licensure. This allows Oklo to begin generating time-sensitive operational data and reduce the risks associated with future deployments. With recent favorable pro-nuclear policies and a well-funded near-term balance sheet consisting of Oklo's $1.4 billion in cash and marketable securities, the company appears ready to move into commercialization. With promising contracts and regulatory strength, I rate this stock a Buy.

Business Overview

Oklo operates as an entirely vertically integrated fission-technology company creating "Aurora powerhouses", which are compact (~1-meter diameter) sodium fast reactors capable of producing electric energy with outputs of up to 75 MW. The reactor designs utilize passive safety systems that will shut down without active intervention. This represents a meaningful difference from the typical design of large light water reactors and removes one of the primary sources of regulatory and operational risk.

While many small modular reactor (SMR) companies are developing similar reactor technologies, Oklo differentiates itself with its fuel strategy. The Oklo-designed "Aurora Powerhouse" may be operated using recycled spent nuclear fuel. The US alone has accumulated more than 90,000 metric tons of this "waste" over several decades of electricity generation. In the long term, if this capability were to become a reality, Oklo believes it would significantly reduce the cost associated with acquiring fuel, providing insulation from uranium price volatility that other traditional nuclear companies cannot provide.

In addition to its commercial power generation activities, Oklo has formally established two additional business units: fuel and isotopes. The fuel unit includes all aspects of its own reactors; however, it also has the potential to evolve into a third-party service provider for the advanced nuclear community. The isotopes unit is still in its early stages but is currently proceeding with the construction of the Groves Test Reactor located in Texas and anticipates criticality by July 4th of this year. High-value isotopes are used in healthcare, defense, and aerospace applications, presenting a highly-profitable ancillary market opportunity that few, if any, of Oklo's nuclear competitors are actively pursuing. This provides optionality to the company and additional upside that competitors don't have.

AI-Driven Power Demand

The growth in data center build-outs will continue to drive Oklo’s commercial opportunities, with US power demand from AI increasing by ~23% annually. Around 2,600 GW of electric generating facilities are now queued to be connected into the existing electrical grid, with a median of five years before being permitted and constructed. This delay between permitting, construction, and connecting the new generating facility into the grid is due to structural limitations within the current system.

Oklo’s behind-the-meter model is designed to address this bottleneck. With its Aurora powerhouse collocated at a data center, customers can obtain reliable, low-cost clean baseload power while bypassing the lengthy interconnection queue. Obtaining this reliable/low cost power is not only an issue of power flexibility, but it may also be the deciding factor in whether a data center can begin operations in 2028 versus 2032. This has resulted in a rapidly growing pipeline of potential customers as they recognize these benefits.

In January 2026, Meta (META) agreed to partner with the company to move forward with developing a 1.2 GW power plant located in Pike County, OH. The planned facility will provide clean energy to support Meta’s Prometheus AI supercluster located in New Albany. The agreement included a prepayment to support the purchase of fuel for the project, representing a solid level of commitment rather than just an expression of interest. Oklo expects the first phase of the facility to come online in 2030.

Prior to this announcement, Switch announced a 12 GW non-binding Master Power Agreement with the company valid through 2044. Before that, Equinix had entered into a letter of intent for 500 MW with a corresponding $25 million prepayment. All in all, the ~14 GW of potential customer commitments represent a demand signal for Oklo’s services that no other nuclear competitors currently have. Given the high-margin economics of these deals, the current valuation could appear insignificant if one or more of these deals materializes.

Regulatory Path and Milestones

Oklo's regulatory strategy is going through the Department of Energy’s (DOE) Reactor Pilot Program instead of the standard NRC licensing pathway. This is another one of the big differences between it and the rest of the SMR industry. Under the DOE’s Pilot Program, Oklo will have the ability to build and operate an Aurora reactor at Idaho National Lab (INL) prior to obtaining commercial licensure by the NRC. This allows the company to obtain operational data from a commercially constructed and operated plant while it works through the rest of its commercial licenses.

In July, Oklo completed the NRC pre-application readiness assessment and stated its intent to submit the first phase of its Combined License Application for the Aurora-INL Reactor during 2025. In November, the DOE approved the Nuclear Safety Design Agreement (NSDA) for the A3F fuel fabrication facility at INL. This was the first time such an agreement had been approved for an advanced nuclear fuel fabrication facility, and the NRC also agreed to expedite review of the company's Principal Design Criteria tropical report. These are key components needed to advance Oklo's licensing progress for its reactor design.

Besides licensing, Oklo has also benefited from regulatory tailwinds, something that should continue given the current demand for clean energy. The ADVANCE Act of 2024 was passed to help remove regulatory barriers associated with the NRC's licensing process for advanced reactors. Also in May 2025, executive orders were issued directing all federal agencies to work together to facilitate faster and easier deployment of nuclear technologies. According to Oklo's CEO, Jacob DeWitte, the regulatory landscape has gone from being a "light tailwind" to a "strong tailwind" in favor of deploying nuclear power plants. This is important to note for a company largely held up by regulatory challenges.

Oklo selected Kiewit as the lead construction contractor for the Aurora-INL project, and expects to have commercial operations online sometime between late 2027 and early 2028. Even at that time, the company will have achieved a substantial head start over almost all other developers of SMRs in the US. Once Oklo has an operating reference plant, it is likely that the licensing pathway will become much easier for future units. This puts them at the center of the clean energy race, with a differentiated solution whose potential seems to be undervalued.

Upcoming Earnings

Oklo reports its Q1 2026 results on May 12, 2026, with expectations of -$0.19 EPS. This is lower than Q4 2025 EPS, which was -$0.27, but is still worse than Q1 2025 EPS of -$0.09 YoY. Given the company's pre-commercial phase, the focus should be on project and regulatory updates rather than financials.

Oklo provided 2026 operating cash guidance of $80-100 million, which represents a ramp-up in personnel growth and project spending. Additionally, management indicated it expects to invest cash of -$350-450 million for other projects across power, fuel, and isotope units. Key project updates to look at include the status of the Aurora-INL timeline, as well as if there is any news regarding the criticality date set forth for the Groves reactor. If the Groves criticality date is moved beyond July 4th, it could cause worsening expectations.

As for funding, with an ATM raise of $1.182 billion completed in January 2026, Oklo's balance sheet provides enough liquidity to cover all near-term project expenses that should extend to other projects as the company advances toward commercialization. Since the end of 2025, shares outstanding have increased ~48% YoY from equity raises made throughout this period, allowing Oklo to invest and pursue opportunities outside its flagship technology, such as isotopes.

Valuation

The 14 GW pipeline of customers represents the maximum amount of total capacity that Oklo could potentially serve today. While the company will not likely realize all of that capacity, as most contracts are non-binding, even modest amounts may be enough. For example, 1-2 GW of operating plants producing power at a cost of $80-100 per MWh under long-term PPAs would still result in significant revenues. 1 GW of production at $90 per MWh and 85% capacity results in approximately $670M in annual revenue. Using EBITDA margins comparable to other nuclear owner/operators, which approach 60-70%, 1 GW produces approximately $400-$470M in EBITDA. If Oklo is able to get just half of its pipeline (7 GW), it equates to ~$3 billion in EBITDA, which is just under one-fourth of its current market cap. Given the long-term potential of nuclear power and strong demand, this could expand the multiple rapidly, especially if Oklo is able to win more contracts.

With the company's net-cash position of ~$1.2 billion, cash value per share is ~$7.07 with the current share count of ~174 million shares. Subtracting this number from current prices of ~$67 implies investors are currently willing to pay approximately $60 per share (or ~$10.5 billion in market cap) for the pipeline and technology related to Oklo. For a company that has received a payment from Meta Inc., signed a construction contract with Kiewit, and is building two demonstration power plants on-site at INL, the current valuation appears to be undervalued. With only modest execution needed to earn large amounts of EBITDA and a short regulatory pathway compared to peers, I believe Oklo is a Buy.

Risks to the Thesis

The main danger to Oklo is that its construction and license will take longer than they expect. The expected timeline for commercial operation (late 2027/ early 2028) is Oklo’s, not the NRC’s. There has been a long history of problems with advanced nuclear projects, including cost overruns and delayed completion; however, the company's Aurora design is smaller and less complex than traditional light water reactors. The DOE pilot program pathway is also much more flexible than the normal NRC process. With this pathway and construction already underway on the Kiewit contract, I see Oklo as having relatively less risk when compared to peers.

The second major risk is that the customer base appears stronger than it is. The Switch Agreement for 12 GW is non-binding, and phase one of the Meta deal does not begin until 2030. If AI capital expenditures are reduced or hyperscalers develop alternative methods to obtain clean power, previous LOIs may be cancelled. While the prepaid structure of both the Meta and Equinix Agreements provides some level of protection against this type of cancellation, it doesn't guarantee that customers will continue with the non-binding agreement.

The company is still pre-revenue, meaning dilution is likely as Oklo works toward commercialization. This has been partially mitigated by current cash and marketable securities numbers totaling $1.4 billion, which should last them around two years (given current guidance of $80-100 million in operating expenses and investing burn of $350-450 million). However, this could be extended over time with the use of project-level debt.

Takeaway

Oklo is an example of a potential high-risk/high-reward infrastructure investment opportunity that benefits from strong AI demand for clean energy. Currently, the company's valuation is based on projected future performance; therefore, investors are buying on the assumption that Oklo will be able to build and license reactors for customers. If the company can deliver on time and convert contracts into revenue, it has the potential to quickly evolve into a primary source of energy for AI-related infrastructure needs. Oklo also has additional optionality through other segments, such as fuel and isotopes, which could help make up losses in the event of nuclear licensing delays. Given these factors and the strong demand for clean energy, I rate this stock a Buy.

data.title: Ucore: Adroit Innovator In REE Tech But Uncertainty Remains
data.description:
Ucore Rare Metals (UURAF) is one of several North American rare earth-related micro-caps—including MP Materials (MP), Critical Metals Corp (CRML), and USA Rare Earth (USAR)—now repositioning themselves amid the growing geopolitical conflict between China and the US over the issue of REE access. Over 80,000 US defense components in 1900 weapons are now dependent on REE oxides subject to Chinese licensing controls, with entire missile pulsion and guidance systems pivoting on what element Beijing bureaucrats will allow export.

Marginal penny stocks before 2024, these firms are now emerging as key players in the West’s (very belated) effort to make from whole cloth a domestic rare earth supply chain within two years. (See prior article on this issue)

Founded in 2006, Ucore struggled for 13 years as an E&P company but has since rallied back as a "refining play." Headquartered in Halifax (and listed in the Toronto exchange—UCU:CA), Ucore initially focused on uranium and rare earth mining, but it has recently evolved into a tech innovator in the REE processing space. The firm is far less known than its peers, though it does have several attributes that could position it for later success:

  • A patent-pending separation method.
  • Funding and distribution contracts from the US Department of Defense.
  • A 10-year offtake agreement with Critical Metals Corp. to receive HREEs from its proposed Tanbreez mine, one of the world’s largest REE deposits.
  • Bokan --ownership of one of the highest-grade HREE sites in the United States.

At the center of Ucore’s viability is RapidSX, a refining technology that matches the output and quality of traditional solvent extraction but significantly improves speed while reducing environmental impact. The process was built with Western high environmental standards in mind (in contrast with China’s historically cavalier approach).

On April 7, the company released a Phase 1 report that touted its superiority compared to existing conventional CSX methods:

This Phase 1 report gives dramatic color to how well UURAF’s tech does in a head-to-head competition. RapidSX provides an extraction rate that is 5.4x faster and a settling rate that is 1.8x faster. There is better operational flexibility (i.e., being turned on and off, targeting only banned REEs, or separating each REE with several different purities), and with a far smaller footprint. It also generates a 34% reduction in CAPEX with much better ESG metrics. The numbers suggest commercial execution can be profitable.

This Phase 1 was formally accepted by the DoD and triggers a payment, as per their 2025 agreement. Last year Ucore secured $18.4 million in funding from the U.S. Army Contracting Command to support construction of its first Strategic Metals Complex in Alexandria, Louisiana, and the groundbreaking took place in May 2025.

Two weeks ago Ucore suggested that it had received and was installing a 4000-gallon leaching reactor, with the transfer of additional technology from their Ontario location. In that press release, the firm stated that it aims for full commercial commission of RapidSX in Louisiana in the first half of 2027.

Crucially, all the plant components—from pumps to piping—of this Alexandria, LA plant are sourced from North American suppliers. The facility has also received DPAS (Defense Priorities & Allocations System) status, ensuring its supply chain is prioritized for national defense needs.

Back in October, China initiated new export controls via intrusive licensing and “regulatory slow walks” on specific HREE products and REE processing-related technology more generally. The licensing actively restricts military or dual-use in order to “safeguard world peace.”

Ucore was the only REE company to quickly respond to the Beijing announcement (probably due to its tech element), stating that it “does not require or intend to use” equipment sourced from China for its U.S.-based heavy rare earth (HREE) separation operations.

In September, Ucore entered a "friend-shoring" partnership with Aussie firm Metallium to create a more streamlined refining process. Metallium will use its Flash Joule Heating (FJH) technology in Houston to convert feedstocks into high-purity chlorides, simplifying the upstream stage while Ucore’s RapidSX system in Louisiana then completes the separation into individual rare earth oxides. Thus, the two aim to build an end-to-end TX-LA refining pathway.

The CRML Partnership & Bokan

In August, Ucore and Critical Metals signed a “letter of intent” for a 10-year supply agreement. Under this deal, CRML will deliver at least 10,000 metric tons annually of rare earth concentrate from its Tanbreez project in Greenland—approximately 10% of the site’s projected output. Most of this will go into the DoD’s supply chain.

As I have discussed in my CRML report, Tanbreez is one of the world’s richest REE deposits, with an estimated 45 million tons of material, 28% of which consists of HREEs. Located near Qaqortoq in Greenland, the site also benefits from low uranium and thorium content, making it environmentally attractive. Its southern all-year access via a deep-water fjord means easy shipping. The mine will have a 25-year lifespan.

Critical Metals expects to complete its pilot plant by May and see production in 3Q 2027. Earlier projections estimate a net present value of roughly $3 billion, with a phased production plan beginning at 85,000 tons annually in 2026 and potentially reaching 425,000 tons by 2030. Ucore is expected to serve as the initial processing partner.

In addition to Tanbreez, Ucore owns the Bokan Mountain project in Alaska, home to the highest-grade HREE deposit in the US. Located on Prince of Wales Island, the site contains significant amounts of dysprosium, terbium, and yttrium—the heavy rare earths that are critical for advanced technologies such as electric vehicles and military systems.

Despite early development efforts, Bokan has remained unmined due to competition from low-cost Chinese supply. However, shifting geopolitical dynamics and rising prices may revive its viability. Earlier estimates suggest a deposit totaling 5.2 million tons, with roughly 40% consisting of heavy rare earths.

Financials & Outlook Issues

Ucore's stock has risen nearly 288% over the past year, even though the company lost US$28.6 million in 2025, far worse than the $9.4 million loss of 2024. The ticker presently has a market cap of roughly $478 million following a sharp speculative surge.

Of course, 2025 was the costly year of the RapidSX buildout -which got done and is now through phase 1—but it suggests how risky this pre-revenue company is. (The company’s quarterly financial disclosures remain unaudited—typically a concern for investors, though the yearly statements are). The company lost US$5.7 million in its 4Q 2025, far better than the $16.3 million loss, which was when the Louisiana buildout was in full swing.

At present, the company has $19 million in cash, with total assets of US$60.9 million versus total liabilities of $9.1 million ($5 of which is short-term borrowing).

Still, the rare earth landscape has changed dramatically in 2025. Ucore and its competitors are now being actively supported by the US government as part of a broader US strategy to reduce reliance on China. The company’s Louisiana facility represents its first real step toward serious production, with a commercial start planned early 2027.

According to the company website, the company will be processing feedstock by mid-2026, perhaps via the Canadian location:

This facility will be commissioned in stages, with production scaling up from 2,000 tons per annum (tps) in 2026 to 5,000 tpa in 2027 and a further potential increase to 7500 tpa in 2028.

Under favorable pricing conditions, profitability by 2028 is plausible, with the company projecting up to $750-800 million in annual revenue by 2030. The CRML off-take agreement to supply 10,000 metric tons of REE a year kicks in, in 2027 and goes for 10 years, with Ucore producing high-purity rare earth oxides from the mixed rare earth carbonates.

According to the August 2025 press release, Ucore’s CEO Pat Ryan declared:

Critical Metal Corps’s Tanbreez offers tremendous opportunities for Ucore given the significant concentration of heavy rare earths it contains, which are essential for our processing facility in Louisiana, and our downstream partners. Both Critical Metals Corp and Ucore share a vision to lessen China’s grip of the rare earth ecosystem in the West, and we look forward to our partnership, positioning us both to meet the growing demand for rare earths while addressing national security challenges.

This Ucore offtake agreement was CRML’s first buyer(!). It shows just how much operational ground has been covered in the past year that Ucore’s plant (initiated in May 2025) is now through Phase 1 and CRML is expected to have a pilot facility set to open next month. (See my Tanbreez article here).

Back in October, the U.S. government explicitly shot down reports that it was considering a stake in CRML. Now, with the clearer full ownership, it may well now invest in CRML as it did with USAR and MP.

The risks to US manufacturing from China's new regime of REE licensing are made explicit in this Ucore slide:

Risks Remain

Ultimately, the investment thesis for Ucore—and the sector as a whole—rests on a single premise: that the U.S. cannot return to dependence on Chinese rare earths and must now rebuild an entire supply chain domestically. Since the first REE embargo in 2010, geostrategic tensions have become far more explicit, and China’s REE dominance has only grown more perilous.

The big questions remain, however, for Ucore investors.

Can this tiny REE refining company find its niche in the growing Western competition --MP, USAR, and Lynas Rare Earths (LYSCF)—especially when a lot of supply comes online by 2027-2028? Yes, it has the DoD contract, but will there be robust commercial demand for all of them?

In addition, these firms will still be coping with that ever-present China price. Even as products from the mainland look to be walled off from Western manufacturers, Chinese REEs may still be periodically dumped into the West to disrupt these companies—just as what happened in 2012-2015. At present, the PRC's new April 2025/October 2025 export bans and the emerging “long-arm jurisdiction” restrict the "leaking" of resources into the West, but that could change overnight if enforcement is lax or targeted dumping appears strategically appealing to Beijing. Also, the Trump administration has appeared to turn away from constructive price floors, though there could be a "preferential critical minerals trade bloc" emerging with allies.

At the same time, Beijing's rapid "Made in China 2025" technological advancements—particularly in areas like EVs, data center AI, humanoid robotics, and aerospace—have dramatically increased its own domestic REE demand. In the coming decade, the mainland may consume most (or all) of its own strategic metals as it simply surpasses the West in both innovation and sheer scale.

Whether Ucore ultimately succeeds will depend not just on execution but on the durability of this new geopolitical reality (which I have described in great detail here).

Ucore has proven itself an adroit innovator, transforming itself into a go-to processing player. The company is worth keeping an eye on, though uncertainty remains for this micro-cap until early 2027. I would not add here, but would rather wait for a later entry point. Profitability will prove elusive until 2028 at the earliest.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Japanese Inflation Quickened In March, Complicating Bank of Japan Outlook
data.description:
Inflation stronger than expected despite government subsidies

Japan’s headline CPI inflation rose 1.5% year-on-year in March (vs. 1.3% in February, 1.4% market consensus), and core inflation excluding fresh food accelerated for the first time in five months to a 1.8% rate (vs. 1.6% in February, 1.5% market consensus). The index increased by 0.4% month-over-month on a seasonally adjusted basis, reflecting increased pressure from goods, which rose by 0.6%, while services also increased by 0.2%.

Looking at the details, prices for fresh food (-4.8%), gasoline (-5.4%), utilities (-4.8%), and education (-5.5%) dropped. Fresh food prices dropped for four consecutive months, mostly due to a high base last year driven by rice prices. The last three price drops were due to government energy subsidies and social welfare programmes. Aside from these items, increases became more widespread with household goods (2.7%), transport (2.1%), and entertainment (2.35%). Although core inflation is below 2% for a second month, excluding policy effects, inflation should stay well above 2%.

Inflation to accelerate further in coming months

We expect further inflation acceleration in the coming months. On top of rising global energy prices, the weak JPY should add more pressure on domestic prices. Producer prices and import prices increased quite sharply, too. Also, businesses usually adjust their retail prices in April, the first month of the fiscal year, which should further accelerate price gains. This year’s “shunto” negotiations resulted in wage growth above 5%, and increases for SMEs are quite firm as well. Thus, businesses are likely to pass these input cost hikes on to consumers.

We expect Tokyo CPI (to be released on May 1)to rise to 1.7% YoY in April (vs. 1.4% in March). We expect both goods and service prices to rise more quickly in April. Also, from May, both headline and core inflation are expected to climb above 2% again. This should support the BoJ's further policy normalisation process. We currently have 50 bp hikes pencilled in by the end of 2026.

BoJ watch

Markets widely expect the BoJ to stay on hold after local wires quoted comments from people familiar with the matter saying the BoJ would not hike in April. We understand that the Middle East situation is fluid and uncertain. But in our view, recent data suggest that energy shocks are having a more prolonged and much larger impact on inflation than on growth. This will be reflected in the BoJ’s upcoming quarterly outlook report next week. We expect the inflation outlook to be revised sharply upward. Meanwhile, downward revision of growth should be limited. That said, this is a non-consensus call, but we still expect the BoJ to make a data-dependent decision on the hike. If the market is right about the BoJ skipping the hike in April, then the BoJ’s communication should give a much stronger signal for a rate hike in June.

Inflation is expected to grow faster, supporting the BoJ's rate hikes in 2H26

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
